title: The last keeper and the new lamp
summary: On a small Scottish island, the daughter of a retiring lighthouse keeper and a coastguard official disagree about what is lost when a lighthouse is fully automated — one thinks of people, the other of systems.

body.speaker: Mr Aldous
body.text: Thank you for meeting me, Catriona. The new lamp goes live on Friday, and after that the station no longer needs a keeper. I wanted to explain in person, not by letter.

body.speaker: Catriona
body.text: My father has kept this light for thirty-one years; his father kept it before him. You have come to tell me a machine will do it better.

body.speaker: Mr Aldous
body.text: Not better. More reliably, more cheaply, and without asking a family to live two hours by boat from a doctor. The new system checks the bulb, the rotation and the backup generator every ninety seconds, and reports any fault to the mainland. A keeper sleeps. The system does not.

body.speaker: Catriona
body.text: A keeper sleeps, yes — but a keeper also wakes. The night the trawler went onto the rocks in 1986, the automatic systems reported the light was working perfectly. It was. But my father had heard the engine note change in the dark and was on the cliff with a rope before the harbour knew. The light told the truth. My father told the whole truth.

body.speaker: Mr Aldous
body.text: I will not pretend the new lamp can hear an engine. It cannot. But the rope on the cliff is not a lighthouse function. Rescue is the coastguard's job now, and with helicopters and satellite tracking we are faster than in 1986.

body.speaker: Catriona
body.text: That is what worries me. You have taken the job apart into pieces — the light, the rescue, the weather — each given to a machine or an office. Each piece works. But my father was where all the pieces met: the one person who could see the light, hear the sea, and know the boats by name, all at once.

body.speaker: Mr Aldous
body.text: You have put your finger on the real change. Modern safety is not one person who knows everything; it is many systems that each know one thing well and pass information between them quickly. Less human, but less likely to fail, because no single tired man is the weak point.

body.speaker: Catriona
body.text: And on the night the systems disagree — when the light says all is well and the sea says otherwise?

body.speaker: Mr Aldous
body.text: Then a duty officer on the mainland looks at the data and makes a judgement, as your father did. We have not removed the human — we have moved him. From the island it looks like an empty house; from the network, it is one more node nobody must risk their life to keep lit.

body.speaker: Catriona
body.text: An empty house is exactly what it will be. I am not asking you to change the decision. I am asking you to write in your report that the house was not empty before — that something was kept here that your ninety-second checks do not measure, and that it is ending, not improving.

body.speaker: Mr Aldous
body.text: I can write that. I think I should. The light will be brighter on Friday and the coast safer; both are true, and so is what you have said. Those can be true together. May I shake your father's hand before the boat?

annotations.meaningVi: đi vào hoạt động / được kích hoạt
tàu kéo lưới / tàu đánh cá

annotations.phrase: on the cliff with a rope
annotations.meaningVi: trên vách đá với một sợi dây
một chức năng của đèn biển

annotations.phrase: taken the job apart into pieces
annotations.meaningVi: chia nhỏ công việc ra thành từng mảnh
đưa ra phán đoán / quyết định
là đang kết thúc, chứ không phải đang được cải thiện

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect for life experience + modal verbs for possibility and obligation
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài dùng hiện tại hoàn thành chỉ kinh nghiệm/khoảng thời gian: 'My father has kept this light for thirty-one years', 'I have read that report', 'You have put your finger on the real change'. Bài cũng dùng động từ khuyết thiếu để diễn đạt khả năng và bổn phận: 'the new lamp can hear an engine. It cannot.' (khả năng), 'a duty officer... makes a judgement' và 'I think I should' (should = nên, bổn phận), 'May I come to the house...?' (may = xin phép lịch sự).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The dialogue uses the present perfect for experience and duration: 'My father has kept this light for thirty-one years', 'I have read that report', 'You have put your finger on the real change'. It also uses modal verbs for possibility and obligation: 'the new lamp can hear an engine. It cannot.' (ability), 'I think I should' (should = ought to / obligation), and 'May I come to the house...?' (may = a polite request for permission).

translationVi:
Mr Aldous: Cảm ơn cô đã gặp tôi, Catriona. Đèn mới sẽ hoạt động vào thứ Sáu, và sau đó trạm không cần người canh nữa. Tôi muốn giải thích trực tiếp, không phải qua thư.

Catriona: Cha tôi đã giữ ngọn đèn này ba mươi mốt năm; trước đó cha của ông ấy giữ. Ông đến để nói với tôi rằng một cái máy sẽ làm tốt hơn.

Mr Aldous: Không phải tốt hơn. Mà đáng tin cậy hơn, rẻ hơn, và không bắt một gia đình phải sống cách bác sĩ hai giờ đi thuyền. Hệ thống mới kiểm tra bóng đèn, sự quay và máy phát dự phòng mỗi chín mươi giây, và báo bất kỳ lỗi nào về đất liền. Người canh thì ngủ. Hệ thống thì không.

Catriona: Người canh ngủ, đúng — nhưng người canh cũng thức dậy. Cái đêm chiếc tàu cá đâm vào đá năm 1986, các hệ thống tự động báo rằng đèn đang hoạt động hoàn hảo. Đúng là vậy. Nhưng cha tôi đã nghe tiếng động cơ đổi khác trong bóng tối và đã ở trên vách đá với sợi dây trước khi bến cảng biết. Ngọn đèn nói sự thật. Cha tôi nói toàn bộ sự thật.

Mr Aldous: Tôi sẽ không vờ rằng đèn mới có thể nghe tiếng động cơ. Nó không thể. Nhưng sợi dây trên vách đá không phải là chức năng của đèn biển. Cứu hộ giờ là việc của tuần duyên, và với trực thăng và theo dõi vệ tinh, chúng tôi nhanh hơn năm 1986.

Catriona: Đó chính là điều làm tôi lo. Ông đã chia công việc thành từng mảnh — đèn, cứu hộ, thời tiết — mỗi mảnh giao cho một cái máy hoặc một văn phòng. Mảnh nào cũng chạy. Nhưng cha tôi là nơi tất cả các mảnh gặp nhau: người duy nhất có thể thấy đèn, nghe biển, và biết tên từng con tàu, tất cả cùng một lúc.

Mr Aldous: Cô đã chỉ trúng vào sự thay đổi thật sự. An toàn hiện đại không phải là một người biết mọi thứ; mà là nhiều hệ thống mỗi cái biết rõ một thứ và chuyển thông tin cho nhau thật nhanh. Ít tính người hơn, nhưng ít khả năng hỏng hơn, vì không có một người mệt mỏi đơn lẻ nào là điểm yếu.

Catriona: Còn vào cái đêm các hệ thống bất đồng — khi đèn nói mọi sự ổn còn biển nói khác?

Mr Aldous: Thì một sĩ quan trực ở đất liền nhìn dữ liệu và đưa ra phán đoán, như cha cô đã làm. Chúng tôi không loại bỏ con người — chúng tôi đã dời con người đi. Từ hòn đảo nhìn ra, nó trông như một ngôi nhà trống; từ mạng lưới nhìn vào, nó là thêm một nút mà không ai phải liều mạng để giữ cho sáng.

Catriona: Một ngôi nhà trống đúng là cái nó sẽ trở thành. Tôi không yêu cầu ông đổi quyết định. Tôi yêu cầu ông viết vào báo cáo rằng ngôi nhà này trước đây không trống — rằng có một thứ đã được giữ ở đây mà những lần kiểm tra chín mươi giây của ông không đo được, và rằng nó đang kết thúc, chứ không phải đang được cải thiện.

Mr Aldous: Tôi có thể viết điều đó. Tôi nghĩ tôi nên. Ngọn đèn sẽ sáng hơn vào thứ Sáu và bờ biển an toàn hơn; cả hai đều đúng, và điều cô nói cũng vậy. Chúng có thể cùng đúng. Tôi bắt tay cha cô trước chuyến thuyền được không?

questions.prompt: Why has Mr Aldous come to the island in person?
options: To explain in person, before the new lamp goes live on Friday, why the station no longer needs a keeper
questions.explanation: 'I wanted to explain the decision in person rather than by letter.'

questions.prompt: What advantages does Aldous claim for the new system?
options: It checks the bulb, rotation and generator every ninety seconds and reports faults instantly; it never sleeps
It can hear engines in the dark
questions.explanation: 'The new system checks the bulb, the rotation, the backup generator... every ninety seconds, and reports any fault to the mainland instantly... The system does not [sleep].'

questions.prompt: What happened on the night of the 1986 trawler?
options: The automatic systems reported the light was fine; her father heard the engine note change and was on the cliff with a rope before the harbour knew
questions.explanation: 'my father had heard the engine note change in the dark and was already on the cliff with a rope before the harbour even knew.'

questions.prompt: What does Catriona mean by 'The light told the truth. My father told the whole truth'?
options: The lamp only reported it was working; her father also knew the boat was in danger
Her father lied about the light
The light gave a false report
Both said exactly the same thing
questions.explanation: The light was 'working perfectly' (true), but her father perceived the real danger the light could not.

questions.prompt: How does Aldous respond to the rescue argument?
options: He admits the lamp cannot hear an engine, but says rescue is now the coastguard's job and they are faster than in 1986
He claims the lamp can perform rescues
He says rescues are no longer needed
questions.explanation: 'the rope on the cliff is not, strictly, a lighthouse function. Rescue is the coastguard's job now, and we are faster than we were in 1986.'

questions.prompt: What is Catriona's deeper objection about 'taking the job apart'?
options: Each piece works, but her father was the place where all the pieces met — one person who saw, heard and knew the boats at once
The pieces do not work at all
There were never any separate pieces
Machines cannot do any of the pieces
questions.explanation: 'my father was the place where all the pieces met. He was the one person who could see the light, hear the sea, and know the boats by name, all at once.'

questions.prompt: How does Aldous describe modern safety?
options: Not one person who knows everything, but many systems each knowing one thing well and passing information quickly — less human, but less likely to fail over time
One expert who replaces all machines
A single tired man watching everything
A return to the methods of 1986
questions.explanation: 'Modern safety is not one person who knows everything; it is many systems that each know one thing very well and pass information between them quickly.'

questions.prompt: When the systems disagree, who decides, according to Aldous?
options: A duty officer on the mainland looks at the data and makes a judgement — the human has been moved, not removed
No one; the machine decides alone
questions.explanation: 'a duty officer on the mainland looks at the data and makes a judgement... We have not removed the human, Catriona. We have moved him.'

options: That his report record the house was not empty before, that something unmeasured is ending, not improving
That he give her father a new job on the mainland
That he keep the old lamp as a museum
questions.explanation: 'I am asking you to write in your report that the house was not empty before... it is ending, not improving.'

questions.prompt: How does Aldous reconcile the two views at the end?
options: He agrees the coast will be safer and that what she said is also true — both can be true together — and offers to shake her father's hand
questions.explanation: 'The light will be brighter on Friday and the coast will be safer... So is what you have said. Those can be true together.'

questions.prompt: In Aldous's image, what does the lighthouse look like 'from the network'?
options: One more node nobody must risk their life to keep lit
A family home full of life
questions.explanation: From the network, it is 'one more node nobody must risk their life to keep lit.'
questions.hint: Ninth turn, the contrast between island and network.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the dialogue?
options: Automation makes the coast measurably safer while genuinely ending something human and unmeasured; both speakers grant the other's point rather than declaring one side right.
Old keepers are simply against progress.
Machines are always better than people.
questions.explanation: Both acknowledge truth: 'Those can be true together.' The disagreement is reframed, not won.

cloze.template:
Mr Aldous: Thank you for meeting me, Catriona. The new lamp goes {{b1}} on Friday, and after that the station no longer needs a keeper. I wanted to explain in person, not by letter.
Catriona: My father has kept this light for thirty-one years; his father kept it before him. You have come to tell me a machine will do it better.
Mr Aldous: Not better. More {{b2}}, more cheaply, and without asking a family to live two hours by boat from a doctor. The new system checks the bulb, the rotation and the backup generator every ninety seconds, and reports any {{b3}} to the mainland. A keeper sleeps. The system does not.
Catriona: A keeper sleeps, yes — but a keeper also wakes. The night the trawler went onto the rocks in 1986, the automatic systems reported the light was working perfectly. It was. But my father had heard the engine {{b4}} change in the dark and was on the cliff with a rope before the harbour knew. The light told the truth. My father told the whole {{b5}}.
Mr Aldous: I will not pretend the new lamp can hear an engine. It cannot. But the rope on the cliff is not a lighthouse function. Rescue is the {{b6}}'s job now, and with helicopters and satellite tracking we are faster than in 1986.
Catriona: That is what worries me. You have taken the job apart into {{b7}} — the light, the rescue, the weather — each given to a machine or an office. Each piece works. But my father was where all the pieces met: the one person who could see the light, hear the sea, and know the boats by name, all at once.
Mr Aldous: You have put your finger on the real change. Modern safety is not one person who knows everything; it is many systems that each know one thing well and {{b8}} information between them quickly. Less human, but less likely to fail, because no single tired man is the weak point.
Catriona: And on the night the systems disagree — when the light says all is well and the sea says otherwise?
Mr Aldous: Then a duty {{b9}} on the mainland looks at the data and makes a judgement, as your father did. We have not removed the human — we have moved him. From the island it looks like an empty house; from the network, it is one more node nobody must risk their life to keep lit.
Catriona: An empty house is exactly what it will be. I am not asking you to change the decision. I am asking you to write in your report that the house was not empty before — that something was kept here that your ninety-second {{b10}} do not measure, and that it is ending, not improving.
Mr Aldous: I can write that. I think I should. The light will be brighter on Friday and the coast safer; both are true, and so is what you have said. Those can be true {{b11}}. May I shake your father's hand before the boat?

blanks.explanation: The new lamp goes live on Friday.
Reports any fault to the mainland.
My father told the whole truth.
Rescue is the coastguard's job now.
Taken the job apart into pieces.
Each knows one thing well and pass information between them.
A duty officer on the mainland.
Your ninety-second checks do not measure it.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Aldous says the human was not removed but 'moved' — from a man on a cliff to a duty officer reading data on the mainland. In a part of your own life that has become automated, where did the human judgement actually go, and is it still close enough when it is needed?
